segments.paradiso-18-g43: E is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this line to the preceding action. Al nome is the prepositional phrase a + il contracted to al, meaning 'at the mention/name of' (used idiomatically: 'at the name'). De l’ is the older form of di + il, 'of the', and l’alto Macabeo is a noun phrase with the epithet alto ('lofty/high') modifying the proper name Macabeo. Vidi + moversi is a classic perception construction: vidi (1st‑person singular past) takes the infinitive moversi (reflexive infinitive) so that 'I saw another move himself'; un altro is the logical subject of the infinitive, and roteando is a present participle/gerund indicating the manner of the movement ('spinning').
segments.paradiso-18-g44: E 'and' resumes the statement. Letizia is the subject noun 'joy'; era is the 3rd‑person singular imperfect of essere 'was' linking the subject to the following predicate; Ferza is given here as a proper name (or substantive predicate) and del is the contraction di + il 'of the', so del paleo is a genitive phrase modifying Ferza. Grammatically this is a copular clause: 'joy was Ferza of the paleo.'
segments.paradiso-18-g45: Così is an adverb ('so/thus') introducing the result. Per Carlo Magno e per Orlando uses per twice meaning 'for' or 'in the case of' to name the two figures; Carlo Magno is the fixed name 'Charlemagne' (Magno = 'the Great'). Due ne is a partitive construction: due 'two' + ne 'of them/of those', and seguì is the 3rd‑person singular past (perfect) 'followed'. Lo mio attento sguardo is the object/phrase performing the following: lo is the archaic/dialectal definite article before mio, mio 'my' + attento 'attentive' qualifying sguardo 'gaze/look'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but reads as 'my attentive gaze followed two of them.'
segments.paradiso-18-g46: Com’ is the contracted form of come ('as' / 'like'), introducing a simile. Occhio is the nominative noun 'eye' which is the subject of segue (present indicative 'follows'); suo is the possessive 'its/his', and falcon is the object 'falcon/hawk'. Volando is the gerund/participle 'flying' describing the manner in which the falcon moves; together the clause is a vivid present‑tense simile: 'as an eye follows its falcon flying.'
segments.paradiso-18-g47: Poscia is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. Trasse is the 3rd‑person singular past historic of trarre 'drew/led' and functions transitively with la mia vista as direct object ('drew my sight'). The coordinated proper names Guiglielmo e Rinoardo e 'l duca Gottifredi form the agents (subject) placed after the verb for poetic inversion. Per quella croce is an adverbial/prepositional phrase ('toward/through/by that cross') indicating the direction or occasion of the action; quella is the demonstrative 'that' and croce 'cross'.
segments.paradiso-18-g48: This short segment is simply the continuation of the list of names: e 'and' coordinates Ruberto Guiscardo, another proper name added to those who 'drew' the speaker's sight. Grammatically it is an appended noun phrase serving as another element of the compound subject or the enumerated agents.
segments.paradiso-18-g49: Indi is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'next', marking a temporal sequence from the previous actions.
segments.paradiso-18-g50: Tra l’altre luci is a prepositional phrase 'among the other lights' (tra = among; l’ + altre luci = 'the other lights'). Mota e mista are adjectival/participial modifiers: mota 'moved' (participial adjective) and mista 'mixed/mingled', joined by e 'and' to describe the state of those lights; adjectives agree in sense with luci and are placed after the noun in this poetic ordering.
segments.paradiso-18-g51: Mostrommi is the 1st‑person singular perfect 'showed me' (mostrare + mi enclitic). L’alma is the direct object 'the soul' shown. Che introduces a relative clause 'that/who', and m’avea parlato is 'had spoken to me' with m’ representing the clitic object pronoun 'mi' ('to me') and avea the imperfect auxiliary 'had' plus parlato (past participle) forming the pluperfect sense 'had spoken'. Qual era tra i cantor del cielo artista is a nominal clause in which qual 'what/which' + era (imperfect of essere) asks/renders what sort of being she was 'among the singers of heaven' (tra i cantor del cielo), artista functioning as a predicate noun 'a master/artist.'
segments.paradiso-18-g1: Già is an adverb meaning “already.” si godeva is a pronominal (middle-voice) imperfect verb meaning “was enjoying” or “rejoiced in” with the reflexive particle si; the subject quello specchio beato (‘that blessed mirror’) is placed after the verb for poetic inversion. del suo verbo is a prepositional phrase modifying what the mirror enjoyed (‘of His Word’).
segments.paradiso-18-g2: e io gustava is a simple imperfect narrative clause, “and I was savoring”; lo mio is a nominal phrase meaning “my own (thing/portion),” with the neutral article lo used poetically to nominalize mio.
segments.paradiso-18-g3: temprando is a present participle/gerund (“tempering, moderating”) that links to gustava and describes the manner in which he tasted his own; col is the contraction of con + il (“with the”), and l’acerbo (‘the bitter/harsh thing’) is the object being tempered by il dolce (‘the sweet’).
segments.paradiso-18-g4: e quella donna introduces a new subject with e + demonstrative 'that lady'; ch’ (che) is the relative pronoun “who”; a Dio mi menava is an imperfect clause meaning “was leading me to God” with menava as the imperfect of menare; disse begins the quoted speech.
segments.paradiso-18-g5: Muta is an imperative (or poetic infinitive used as a command) meaning “change” or “shift”; pensier is a poetic form of pensiero, here the direct object meaning “thought” — so “Change your thought.”
segments.paradiso-18-g6: pensa is an imperative second-person singular “think”; ch’ introduces the clause that follows. i’ (archaic spelling of io) sono presso a colui is ‘I am near to him’; the second ch’ is a relative pronoun “who”; ogne torto disgrava is a relative clause with disgrava (imperfect) meaning something like “made light of” or “remitted” every wrong — an older verb meaning to relieve or treat a wrong as nothing.
segments.paradiso-18-g7: Io mi rivolsi is a past-tense (perfect/simple past/imperfect depending on editorial tradition) reflexive verb meaning “I turned (myself)” or “I addressed myself”; a + l’amoroso suono is the prepositional object ‘to the loving sound’; del mio conforto is a genitive/partitive phrase ‘of my comfort/encouragement.’
segments.paradiso-18-g8: e qual io allor vidi is a comparative/illustrative clause introduced by qual (here “just as” or “such as”) and vidi (past of vedere) “I saw”; ne li occhi santi amor means literally “Love in her holy eyes,” with ne = in and li occhi santi = the holy eyes — Amor is personified and the object seen.
segments.paradiso-18-g9: qui l’abbandono places the idea ‘abbandono’ (giving oneself up/abandonment/letting go) at this point: qui ‘here/at this point’ + the article l’ makes it a substantive phrase; the colon that follows announces the explanation that comes next.
segments.paradiso-18-g10: non perch’ is short for non perché ‘not because’; io pur del mio parlar diffidi literally ‘I even/duly distrust my own speech’: pur is emphatic (‘even/nevertheless’), del mio parlar is ‘of my speaking/words,’ and diffidi is present subjunctive/indicative form meaning ‘I distrust’ in this negated causal clause.
segments.paradiso-18-g11: ma per la mente che non può redire sovra sé tanto is the real reason: ma ‘but’; per la mente ‘by/for the mind’; che introduces a relative clause describing the mind: non può redire ‘cannot return’ (redire here = go back, retrace or reflect) sovra sé tanto ‘so far upon itself’ — i.e., the mind cannot turn back upon itself to that extent.
segments.paradiso-18-g12: s’altri non la guidi is a conditional clause where s’ is the contracted se ‘if’; altri is ‘another/someone else’; non la guidi uses the present subjunctive guidi (3rd person) after se in a negative conditional sense: “if another does not guide it.”
segments.paradiso-18-g13: Tanto poss’ io (poetic contraction of 'posso') di quel punto ridire literally “So much can I recount of that moment”: Tanto 'so much,' poss' is an archaic contracted form of posso/possi ‘I can,’ io is subject, and di quel punto ridire is the infinitive complement ‘to recount of that point/moment.’
segments.paradiso-18-g14: che is the conjunction 'that' introducing the result or content clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-18-g15: rimirando is a present participle/gerund of rimirare ‘to behold/admire,’ here linking to the following main clause (‘contemplating her’); lei is the object pronoun ‘her’ (referring to Beatrice).
segments.paradiso-18-g16: lo mio affetto libero fu da ogne altro disire is the main result clause: lo mio affetto ‘my affection’ (lo used nominally) + libero fu is the perfect/imperfect (poetic past) of essere ‘was free’ + da ogne altro disire ‘from every other desire’ (da = from, ogne altro = every other).
segments.paradiso-18-g17: fin che introduces a temporal clause ‘until’: fin che ’l piacere etterno means ‘until the eternal pleasure,’ with che here as a relative/complementizer and etterno an archaic spelling of eterno.
segments.paradiso-18-g18: che diretto raggiava in Bëatrice is a relative clause modifying piacere etterno: che = ‘which’; diretto is a past participle/adjective 'directed' or 'directly' (here adjectival sense), and raggiava (imperfect) ‘was radiating’ or ‘shone’ in Bëatrice (in = in/within).
segments.paradiso-18-g19: dal bel viso mi contentava col secondo aspetto: dal = dalla (da + il) ‘from her fair face’; mi contentava is imperfect ‘was satisfying me’ or ‘contented me’; col secondo aspetto ‘with the second look/aspect’ explains how the pleasure was communicated (col = con + il).
segments.paradiso-18-g20: Vincendo me is a present participle/gerundive phrase ‘conquering/overcoming me’ that modifies the main clause; col lume d’un sorriso uses the contraction col = con + il and d’un = di + un to mean ‘with the light of a smile’ (lume = light, here figurative).
segments.paradiso-18-g21: ella mi disse is a straightforward past-tense report clause: ella ‘she’ + mi ‘to me’ + disse ‘said’ introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.paradiso-18-g22: Volgiti e ascolta are imperatives addressed to the poet: volgiti (reflexive imperative ‘turn yourself’) and ascolta (‘listen’). The reflexive -ti marks the motion of turning toward something.
segments.paradiso-18-g23: ché non pur ne’ miei occhi è paradiso: ché = 'for' or 'that' introducing the explanation; non pur = 'not only' (pur is emphatic 'only/even'); ne’ miei occhi = 'in my eyes'; è paradiso = 'is paradise.' The clause denies exclusivity: paradise is not solely in her eyes.
segments.paradiso-18-g70: E is the simple conjunction 'and'. Come introduces a simile ('as'). Augelli is the plural noun 'birds'. Surti is an archaic past participle/adjectival form (from surgere) meaning 'having risen' and follows the noun; di rivera uses di + noun to mark origin ('from the riverbank').
segments.paradiso-18-g71: Quasi here means 'as if'. Congratulando is a present participle/gerund ('rejoicing' or 'expressing joy'), modifying the birds; a lor pasture literally 'to/for their pasture' (a + the possessive lor) indicates the object/cause of their rejoicing.
segments.paradiso-18-g72: Fanno is the 3rd person plural present of fare ('they make/form'). Di sé is a reflexive phrase 'of themselves' or 'from themselves' indicating the shape comes from their own bodies. Or ... or is a poetic repetition meaning 'now ... now ...'; tonda and altra are adjectives agreeing with the feminine noun schiera, meaning 'round' and 'another/other', and schiera is 'array/host'.
segments.paradiso-18-g73: Sì links back to the simile meaning 'so' or 'thus'. Dentro ai (dentro + ai) means 'within the' (the preposition inside + the article). Lumi (from lumo) means 'lights' (celestial or luminous points). Sante creature is 'holy creatures'. Volitando is a gerund meaning 'flitting' or 'hovering', and cantavano is the imperfect 'they sang' describing the ongoing action.
segments.paradiso-18-g74: E = 'and'. Faciensi is an archaic/reflexive 3rd person plural form of farsi (here 'they made themselves' or 'they became'), used poetically; the phrase continues with the enumeration of letters they form. Or D means 'now a D' (or = 'now').
segments.paradiso-18-g75: Or = 'now'. I here is a grapheme (the letter I) formed by the creatures; although the listed lemma senses might suggest the pronoun, in context this is the letter being named as one of the shapes.
segments.paradiso-18-g76: Or = 'now' again; L names the letter L formed by the creatures. In sue figure means 'in their forms' (sue is the possessive agreeing with the feminine plural figure). The whole segment completes the series 'now D, now I, now L' as shapes.
segments.paradiso-18-g77: Prima is an adverb meaning 'first' or 'at first', marking the initial phase of the action.
segments.paradiso-18-g78: Cantando is the gerund/participle 'singing', giving the manner of the action—'singing, they ...' and attaches to the subject of the sentence.
segments.paradiso-18-g79: A sua nota: a = 'to' or 'toward', sua nota = 'their/its note' (musical or characteristic sound). Moviensi is an archaic reflexive form (3rd plural) of muoversi/movere meaning 'they moved themselves' or 'they moved (toward it)'; the form is poetically archaic but functions as a simple past/iterative motion here.
segments.paradiso-18-g80: Poi is 'then' or 'afterwards', signaling the next action in sequence.
segments.paradiso-18-g81: Diventando is a gerund 'becoming'; l’un (l' + un) = 'one (of them)' and di questi segni = 'of these signs' — the whole gerundial clause means 'one of these signs becoming' or 'when one of these signs took shape'.
segments.paradiso-18-g82: Un poco = 'a little/for a short while'. S’arrestavano is the imperfect reflexive 'they paused/stopped' (s’ = si). E = 'and'. Taciensi is an archaic reflexive form meaning 'they kept silent' or 'they fell silent' (parallel imperfect/continuous sense).
segments.paradiso-18-g83: This is a vocative invocation: 'O' is the exclamatory particle calling the addressee. 'diva Pegasëa' names the addressee ('Pegasean goddess/Muse'); 'che' is a relative pronoun introducing a relative clause describing her. 'li ’ngegni' uses the article + noun (li = the) with a poetic form of 'ingegni' (talents/ingenia) as the object of the verbs that follow. 'fai' is 2nd person singular present indicative of 'fare' (= 'you make'), followed by the adjective 'glorïosi' as predicative complement. 'e rendili' coordinates a second verb: 'rendili' = rende + li (make them) where the clitic 'li' attaches to the verb; 'longevi' is an adjective (long‑lived) modifying the object. Note the compressed, poetic word order and attached clitics.
segments.paradiso-18-g84: 'ed' is the conjunction 'and' (e before a vowel). 'essi' is a pronoun referring back to the things just described (here 'the minds' or 'those gifts'); 'teco' = 'with you' (prepositional pronoun). 'le cittadi e ’ regni' lists two nouns (the cities and the realms/kingdoms): 'le' is the feminine plural article for 'cittadi' (a medieval plural form meaning cities), and 'regni' is coordinated by 'e'. Word order places the prepositional phrase 'teco' between the subject pronoun and the nouns for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-18-g85: 'illustrami' is an imperative (or hortatory) with enclitic 'mi' (illicitly written as one word): 'illustra' (enlighten/explain) + 'mi' = 'enlighten me' / 'make me clear'. 'di te' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'about you' or 'concerning you'. The verb is addressing the Muse and asking for illumination about herself.
segments.paradiso-18-g86: 'sì ch’' (sì che) introduces a purposive/result clause: 'so that / in such a way that'. 'io' is the subject 'I'. 'rilevi' is subjunctive or present infinitive form of 'rilevare' used here in the sense 'bring out, set forth' (poetic use). 'le lor figure' = 'their figures/forms' with 'le' the article and 'lor' the archaic genitive 'their'. 'com’ io l’ho concette' is a comparative/relative clause: 'as I have conceived them' — here 'com'' = 'come', 'io' subject, 'l’' = them (clitic before auxiliary), 'ho concette' = 'I have conceived' (past tense, participial notion). The clause explains the standard by which the poet will 'bring out' the figures.
segments.paradiso-18-g87: This is an exclamatory wish. 'paia' is a verb form of 'parere' in optative/subjunctive sense ('may it appear / may it seem'); it governs the following noun phrase. 'tua possa' is best read as 'your power' (tua = your; 'possa' here a substantive 'potenza/possa' in medieval usage) so the phrase asks that 'your power' be manifest. 'in questi versi brevi' is a locative phrase 'in these short verses'. The overall construction is a short prayer: 'may your power be evident in these brief verses.'
segments.paradiso-18-g88: 'Mostrarsi' is a reflexive infinitive or impersonal infinitive 'to show/appear (themselves)'; here it's used impersonally ('they show themselves' / 'they appear'). 'dunque' = therefore/then. 'in cinque volte sette' is a numerical expression 'in five times seven' (i.e., 35). 'vocali e consonanti' lists the two categories (vowels and consonants); the order and apposition is typical of poetic cataloguing.
segments.paradiso-18-g89: 'e io notai' = 'and I noted' (io subject + passato remoto 'notai' = I noticed/recorded). 'le parti sì' = 'the parts thus' or 'the parts in this way': 'sì' is an adverb meaning 'thus/so'. The clause states the poet's observation/analysis of the structure just described.
segments.paradiso-18-g90: 'come' = 'as' or 'just as'. 'mi' = dative 'to me' with the verb 'parver' (past form of 'parere' used impersonally here: 'seemed to me'). 'dette' is a feminine plural past participle 'said' modifying 'le parti' (the parts) — syntactically it gives 'as the parts seemed to me to have been said' or 'as they seemed to me to have been stated'. The clause therefore qualifies the poet's report.
segments.paradiso-18-g91: This is a Latin inscription in the vocative/imperative form: DILIGITE (2nd-person plural imperative) = “love” or “cherish,” followed by IUSTITIAM (accusative) = “justice/righteousness.” Dante quotes the Latin command as a unit, so the phrase functions as a quoted motto.
segments.paradiso-18-g92: Primai is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning “first” or “at first.” Fur is an older past-tense form (third-person plural) meaning “they were.” Verbo e nome is a coordinate noun phrase “word and name”; di tutto ’l dipinto = “of the whole painting,” with ’l a contraction of il. The word-order places the quoted motto as the subject (understood) and says it served as both the word and the title of the entire depiction.
segments.paradiso-18-g93: Another Latin inscription quoted: QUI (here) + IUDICATIS (2nd-person plural present or vocative participial sense = “you judge” / “you who judge”) + TERRAM (accusative = “the earth”/“the world”). The line is a second motto quoted literally.
segments.paradiso-18-g94: Fur (see above) = “they were.” Sezzai is a rare/archaic poetic form meaning roughly “set” or “placed” (i.e., placed in the second position); here it indicates that the second quoted motto occupied the next position. The form is poetic/archaic and functions as a past action in agreement with fur.
segments.paradiso-18-g95: Poscia (poi) = “afterward/then.” Ne l’emme del vocabol quinto = “in the M's of the fifth word” (emme = the letter M); del = di + il. Rimasero (passato remoto) = third-person plural “they remained” and ordinate is a past participle used adjectivally = “arranged/ordered.” The whole describes the letters M within the fifth word remaining in an ordered pattern.
segments.paradiso-18-g96: Sì che introduces a result clause = “so that.” Giove pareva = “Jupiter seemed” (pareva = imperfect of parere, indicating appearance). Argento lì d’oro distinto = literal order “silver there, distinguished from gold,” with d’ = di. Distinto is a past participle/adjective meaning “distinct/ distinguished (from).” Word order emphasizes the visual effect: Jupiter appearing as silver, contrasted with gold.
segments.paradiso-18-g97: E vidi scendere = “and I saw (them) descend;” vidi is passato remoto first-person singular of vedere. Scendere is the infinitive expressing what was seen (I saw [them] descend). Altre luci is the direct object/subject of the infinitive = “other lights.” Dove era il colmo de l’emme = relative locative clause “where the summit/top of the M was,” with era (imperfect) describing location.
segments.paradiso-18-g98: E lì quetarsi cantando = “and there to quiet themselves, singing.” Quetarsi is a reflexive infinitive (quetare + si) meaning “to quiet/compose themselves”; here it continues the infinitival idea tied to vidi (I saw them descend and there quiet themselves). Cantando is a gerund/participle “singing,” an adverbial circumstantial participle describing how they quiet themselves.
segments.paradiso-18-g99: Credo is a parenthetical present-tense first-person “I believe/I think,” softening the assertion that follows and marking some uncertainty about the interpretation of the lights' action.
segments.paradiso-18-g100: Il ben = “the good” (a nominalized idea). Ch’ (che) = “that/which.” A sé = “to itself” or “to itself/himself,” where sé is a reflexive pronoun; le is the feminine plural clitic referring back to luci (“them”) and is the object of move. Move (present indicative) = “moves.” Together: “the good that moves them to itself,” i.e. the goodness that draws the lights inward toward itself.
segments.paradiso-18-g101: 'Poi' is a temporal adverb meaning 'then'/'afterwards', introducing the next sequence in the narrative. It stands alone here as a sentence adverb linking to the previous description.
segments.paradiso-18-g102: 'come' = 'as/like', introducing a simile. 'nel percuoter d’i ciocchi arsi' = 'in the striking of the burning logs' where 'nel' = in + article, 'percuoter' = infinitive 'to strike' used substantively/gerundive, 'd’i' = 'dei' (of the) contracted, 'ciocchi arsi' = 'burnt logs/blocks' (ciocchi = logs; arsi = burned/scorched). 'surgono' is 3rd person plural present 'they rise' and 'innumerabili faville' = 'innumerable sparks'. The clause sets up the image of sparks flying when logs are struck.
segments.paradiso-18-g103: 'onde' here = 'from which' / 'whence' introducing a consequence or remark derived from the previous image. 'li stolti' = 'the fools' (li + stolti, with stolti used as noun). 'sogliono' is present 'are wont' / 'tend to', and 'agurarsi' is a reflexive infinitive 'to make omens / to draw auguries' — together: 'from which fools are wont to draw omens'.
segments.paradiso-18-g104: 'resurger parver' is an archaic ordering where the infinitive 'resurger' (to rise again / reappear) is followed by 'parver' (they seemed): 'there seemed to reappear' or 'seemed to rise again'. 'quindi' = 'from there/thereupon'. 'più di mille luci' = 'more than a thousand lights' (più di = more than). 'e salir' attaches another infinitive 'and to ascend' — the sequence describes many lights appearing and ascending. Note the poetic inversion and the use of infinitives with 'parver' to report the seeming action.
segments.paradiso-18-g105: 'qual assai e qual poco' is a comparative distributional phrase: 'some (rise) much and some little' — 'qual' functions like 'some'/'somewhat', 'assai' = 'much/very', 'poco' = 'little'. It qualifies the manner/degree in which the lights ascend.
segments.paradiso-18-g106: 'sì come' = 'just as'. '’l sol' is the poetic contraction of 'il sole' (the sun); here the provided list lacks that sense, so treat 'sol' as 'sun'. 'che l’accende sortille' = 'that kindles them and makes them come forth' where 'l’' is the clitic object referring to the sparks/lights, 'accende' = 'kindles/sets alight', and 'sortille' (from sortire) = 'come forth/issue' — the clause completes the simile comparing the lights' behavior to the action of the sun.
segments.paradiso-18-g107: 'e quïetata ciascuna in suo loco' = 'and, each having been calmed in its own place' where 'quïetata' is a past participle (quieted/calmed), 'ciascuna' = each one, 'in suo loco' = in its own place. The participle phrase explains that, after rising, each light quiets into its assigned position.
segments.paradiso-18-g108: 'la testa e ’l collo d’un’aguglia vidi rappresentare a quel distinto foco' = 'I saw the head and the neck of an eagle represented in that distinct fire.' 'vidi' is passato remoto 'I saw', followed by an infinitive 'rappresentare' (to represent) used as a catenative construction: 'I saw ... represented'. 'd’un’ aguglia' = 'of an eagle' (aguglia = eagle in medieval spelling), 'a quel distinto foco' = 'in that distinct flame' (a + determiner = locative). Word order places the object ('la testa e 'l collo') before the verb for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-18-g24: 'Come si vede' is an impersonal expression: 'as one sees' or 'as is seen'; 'qui' places it 'here' (in this place/passage); 'alcuna volta' = 'sometimes'. The noun phrase 'l'affetto ne la vista' literally 'the affection in the sight' means an emotion visible in the look/eyes (ne = 'in').
segments.paradiso-18-g25: 'S’' is the contracted form of 'se' (if) introducing a conditional; 'elli' (here) stands for 'egli/egli' (referring back to the feeling or person) and is the conditional subject; 'è' is present indicative 'is'; 'tanto' modifies degree 'so much/so strong'.
segments.paradiso-18-g26: 'che' introduces the result clause ('that'); 'da lui' gives the agent/source ('from him' — i.e. from that feeling); 'sia' is present subjunctive of 'essere' used in a result clause after such a conditional; 'tutta l'anima tolta' = 'the whole soul taken away' where 'tolta' is a past participle used in a passive/result sense.
segments.paradiso-18-g27: 'così' ('thus/so') draws the parallel; 'nel fiammeggiar' is a gerundial/nominal form meaning 'in the flaming/blazing' (a poetic infinitive/gerund); 'del folgór santo' is a genitive: 'of the holy lightning' (folgór = archaic form of 'fulgore/fulgor').
segments.paradiso-18-g28: 'a ch’io mi volsi' = 'at which I turned (myself)'; 'a' here = 'to/at', 'ch’' = relative 'that/which', 'io' subject 'I', 'mi volsi' is first person singular past (perfect) of 'volgersi/volgere' meaning 'I turned (myself)'.
segments.paradiso-18-g29: 'conobbi' is a past tense 'I perceived/recognized' (here perfect of 'conoscere' used in narrative); 'la voglia in lui' = 'the desire in him' (lui = that light/being); 'di ragionarmi' = 'to reason/speak with me' where the infinitive 'ragionare' takes the clitic 'mi' as object; 'ancora alquanto' = 'still/a little more' (a small further amount of time).
segments.paradiso-18-g30: 'El' is an archaic subject pronoun 'he'; 'cominciò' is past indicative 'began'. Together they introduce direct speech.
segments.paradiso-18-g31: The locative phrase 'In questa quinta soglia de l'albero' = 'on/in this fifth threshold of the tree'; 'che vive de la cima' is a relative clause 'which lives/has life from the top' (archaic 'de' = 'di'); 'e frutta sempre e mai non perde foglia' uses 'frutta' (verb 'bears fruit') with 'sempre' (always) and the reinforced negative 'mai non' = 'never' + 'perde foglia' = 'loses leaves'.
segments.paradiso-18-g32: 'spiriti son beati' = 'spirits are blessed'; 'son' is the contracted present of 'sono' (are). 'beati' is an adjective 'blessed' (here attributive), not a verb form.
segments.paradiso-18-g33: 'che giù' begins a subordinate clause: 'who down below...' — 'che' = 'who/which', 'giù' = 'down/below' (locative adverb).
segments.paradiso-18-g34: 'prima che venissero al ciel' = 'before they came to heaven'; 'prima che' requires the subjunctive in this temporal/past context, so 'venissero' is the imperfect subjunctive of 'venire' ('they came/were to come').
segments.paradiso-18-g35: 'fuor di gran voce' is an idiomatic expression: 'fuor' = 'out' but here means 'with/outburst of', so 'with a great voice' = 'with loud cries' ('gran voce' = loud/strong voice).
segments.paradiso-18-g36: 'sì ch’ogne musa ne sarebbe opima' is a result clause: 'sì che' = 'so that'; 'ogne musa' = 'every muse' (poetic); 'ne' = 'of it/from it' (partitive, referring to the loud voice); 'sarebbe' is conditional 'would be'; 'opima' = 'rich/fertile/enriched' (metaphorical).
segments.paradiso-18-g37: 'Però mira' is an injunction: 'Therefore, look!' ('mira' is imperative or present used as an address); 'ne’ corni de la croce' = 'in the horns/arms of the cross' ('ne' = in, 'corni' = points/arms, archaic usage).
segments.paradiso-18-g38: 'quello ch’io nomerò' = 'that which I will name/call'; 'quello' = demonstrative 'that', 'ch’io nomerò' is a relative clause with future 'nomerò' ('I will name').
segments.paradiso-18-g39: 'lì farà l’atto' = 'there will make/perform the act' ('farà' = future of 'fare'); the following relative clause 'che fa in nube il suo foco veloce' = 'that his swift fire does/produces in a cloud' where 'fa' is present 'does/makes' and 'il suo foco veloce' = 'his/its swift fire' (foco = fire/ardor).
segments.paradiso-18-g40: 'Io vidi' = 'I saw' (simple past); 'per la croce' = 'along/by the cross' (movement or location along the cross); 'un lume tratto' = 'a light drawn' ('tratto' a past participle used as 'drawn/derived'); 'dal nomar Iosuè' = 'from the naming/name Joshua' ('nomar' archaic 'nomare' = to name).
segments.paradiso-18-g41: 'com’ el si feo' = 'as he himself made/did it'; 'com’' = 'come/as', 'el' = 'he', 'si' is reflexive/emphatic 'himself', and 'feo' is an archaic past form of 'fare' meaning 'did/made'. The clause qualifies how the light was produced.
segments.paradiso-18-g42: 'né mi fu noto' = 'nor was it known to me' ('né' = nor; 'mi' indirect object 'to me'; 'fu noto' = 'was known'); 'il dir' = 'the saying/word' (gerundial/noun use); 'prima che ’l fatto' = 'before the deed' ('prima che' + nominal 'il fatto' = prior to the action).
segments.paradiso-18-g52: Io mi rivolsi is passato remoto (rivolsi) first‑person with the reflexive clitic mi: “I turned (myself).” Dal is the contraction da + il giving “from the,” and dal mio destro lato is a spatial phrase “from my right side.” Per + infinitive (per vedere) expresses purpose: “in order to see/observe” (here “to look toward” Beatrice) and il mio dovere is the direct object meaning “my duty/obligation.”
segments.paradiso-18-g53: The paired o ... o is the correlative “either ... or.” Per parlare is purpose “to speak,” and per atto uses per + noun to mean “by/through an act” — here best read as “by a sign/gesture.” Both infinitival constructions give alternative ways of fulfilling the duty mentioned before.
segments.paradiso-18-g54: Se gnato is a past participle (segnare → segnato) used adjectivally in apposition to atto: “a sign/act, marked” or “a sign already made.” The semicolon separates this explanatory phrase from what follows.
segments.paradiso-18-g55: E vidi is the narrative passato remoto “and I saw.” Le sue luci is the direct object: “her lights” (Beatrice’s lights/lamps/emanations). Tanto + adjective (tanto mere) is an intensifier “so/so very”; here mere is the poetic adjective from Latin merus meaning “pure/undiluted,” describing the quality of the lights.
segments.paradiso-18-g56: Another intensified adjective phrase parallel to the previous one: tanto gioconde repeats the intensifier tanto and uses gioconde (feminine plural to agree with luci) meaning “smiling / joyful.” It continues the descriptive series modifying luci.
segments.paradiso-18-g57: Che here introduces a result clause: “so ... that …” La sua sembianza is the subject of vinceva (vinceva is imperfect indicative, “was prevailing/was surpassing/outshining” — an ongoing past action). Vinceva takes li altri e l’ultimo solere as its objects: “it outshone the others and the ultimo solere.” L’ultimo solere is an archaic/poetic expression (context: celestial lights); the precise meaning is literary/ambiguous.
segments.paradiso-18-g58: E come is a simple connective “and as” (= “and just as”), introducing a simile that follows.
segments.paradiso-18-g59: Per + infinitive (per sentir) gives purpose or reason here: “in order to feel/so as to feel.” Sentir is used in the sense “to feel/experience,” più dilettanza = “more delight,” and bene operando is a gerund phrase (“by/while doing good”), describing the manner that produces that increased delight.
segments.paradiso-18-g60: L’uom di giorno in giorno is an idiom meaning “man day by day” (di giorno in giorno = “from day to day”). S’accorge is present indicative reflexive (egli/one notices/realizes) and introduces the content clause che la sua virtute avanza (“that his virtue advances”), with avanza in the present indicative meaning “progresses/advances.”
segments.paradiso-18-g61: Sì m’accors’ io is a poetic contraction of mi accorsi io (passato remoto) meaning “thus I perceived/realized.” Girare intorno (girare + intorno) is “turning around,” and col cielo insieme (with the sky together) indicates that his own turning had been occurring together with the celestial motion. Avea cresciuto l’arco uses avea (imperfect of avere) + past participle cresciuto to express a prior increase (“had grown/enlarged the arc”) — a perfective sense in narrative.
segments.paradiso-18-g62: Veggendo is the gerund of vedere, “beholding” or “seeing.” Quel miracol is the object “that miracle/wonder,” and più addorno is a comparative phrase where addorno (participle/adjective from addornare) means “more adorned/illustrious,” so the whole phrase says he beheld that miracle rendered yet more splendid.
segments.paradiso-18-g63: Qual è 'l trasmutare is an interrogative/comparative formula meaning “what is the transformation” or “as when the transformation is” — here introducing a simile. In picciol varco di tempo = “in a small opening/passage of time” i.e. “in a short span of time”; varco means a passage/interval.
segments.paradiso-18-g64: Quando introduces the temporal clause “when.” ’L volto suo si discarchi uses the present subjunctive discarchi (from discarcare) in a temporal clause — a common poetic choice that renders the event as a general or potential moment: “when her face is relieved/unloaded.” Di vergogna il carco literally “the burden of shame” (carco = burden).
segments.paradiso-18-g65: Tal fu ne li occhi miei is a compact result: tal fu = “such was,” ne = partitive/locative “in,” li occhi miei = “my eyes.” The verb fu is passato remoto “was.” The whole means “such was (the effect) in my eyes.”
segments.paradiso-18-g66: Quando fui vòlto: fui vòlto is passato remoto passive (or deponent reflexive sense) “when I was turned/when I was made to turn,” indicating the moment when his gaze was directed.
segments.paradiso-18-g67: Per lo candor de la temprata stella sesta: per + noun gives causal sense “because of/on account of.” Lo candor = “the whiteness/brightness,” de la temprata stella = “of the tempered star,” and sesta is the ordinal “sixth,” so the phrase names the cause: the brightness of the tempered sixth star.
segments.paradiso-18-g68: Che dentro a sé m’avea ricolto is a relative clause modifying stella sesta: che = “which,” dentro a sé = “within itself,” m’avea ricolto = imperfect of avere + past participle ricolto, “had gathered/collected me” (i.e. had enfolded me). The clitic m’ is the object “me.”
segments.paradiso-18-g69: Io vidi is passato remoto “I saw.” In quella giovïal facella locates the sight “in that jovial little torch / light” (facella = small flame/torch/light); lo sfavillar de l’amor che lì era is the subject of the following infinitive: “the sparkling of the love that was there.” Segnare (infinitive) functions as a result/complement meaning “to mark/indicate,” and a li occhi miei nostra favella means “our speech to my eyes” (i.e. the sparkle signaled their words to his eyes).
segments.paradiso-18-g109: 'Quei' is a demonstrative/pronominal form meaning 'the one' or 'he who' and begins a relative clause; 'che' is the relative pronoun 'who/which'; 'dipinge' is present indicative of dipingere ('paints/portrays'); 'lì' is the adverb 'there' placed after the verb. Together they mean 'the one who paints there.'
segments.paradiso-18-g110: The clause uses the existential/indefinite pattern 'non ha chi + [subjunctive]' = 'has no one who ...'. 'non' negates, 'ha' is present of avere ('has'), 'chi' is an indefinite relative 'anyone/who', '’l' is the clitic object pronoun (lo) 'him/it' attached before the verb, and 'guidi' is 3rd-person singular present subjunctive of guidare ('to guide') — subjunctive is used after 'chi' in this construction to express 'someone to do X'.
segments.paradiso-18-g111: 'ma' = 'but'; 'esso' is an emphatic third-person pronoun ('he/it himself') used for contrast; 'guida' is present indicative of guidare ('guides'). The clause contrasts the lack of an external guide with the subject's acting as guide (he guides [on his own]).
segments.paradiso-18-g112: 'e' = 'and'; 'da lui' marks the agent in a passive/impersonal construction ('by him' or 'from him'); 'si' is the impersonal/passive clitic, and 'rammenta' is 3rd-person singular ('is remembered' / 'is recalled' or 'remembers' in reflexive contexts) — here the construction means 'is recalled from/by him.' 'quella virtù' is the subject of 'si rammenta'; 'ch’ è forma per li nidi' is a relative clause: 'which is the form for the nests' with 'è' = present of essere and 'per' = 'for'/'with respect to'.
segments.paradiso-18-g113: The phrase is the subject: 'L’' is the definite article ('the'), 'altra' = 'other/another', and 'bëatitudo' is an archaic/Latinized form meaning 'beatitude' or 'blessedness' (poetic noun).
segments.paradiso-18-g114: This relative clause modifies 'L'altra bēatitudo.' 'che' = 'which'; 'contenta' is adjectival (agreeing with bēatitudo) meaning 'content/satisfied'; 'pareva' is imperfect 'seemed'; 'prima' = 'at first'; 'd’' = contraction of 'di' introducing the infinitive; 'ingigliarsi' is a pronominal verb 'to get entangled/ensnared' (here reflexive: 'to entangle itself'); 'a l’emme' = 'in/to the M' (the letter M), with 'a' + definite article 'l'' before 'emme' (the name/letter). The whole gives: 'which at first seemed content to entangle itself in the M.'
segments.paradiso-18-g115: 'con poco moto' is an ablative/advantage phrase 'with little motion' (i.e., with little movement); 'seguitò' is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of seguire and here means 'followed' or 'continued'; 'la 'mprenta' = 'the imprint/impression' (archaic spelling with the initial i elided and indicated by an apostrophe). The clause says the beatitude followed the imprint with only a small movement.
segments.paradiso-18-g116: 'O' is the vocative/exclamatory particle ('O' = 'Oh'); 'dolce stella' is a direct address ('sweet star') — a poetic apostrophe to a guiding star (likely Venus).
segments.paradiso-18-g117: 'quali e quante' = 'what kinds and how many' (parallel interrogative/exclamatory words referring to variety and number); 'gemme' is the subject 'gems' (here, lights/bright points). 'mi dimostraro' is passato remoto (3rd pl.) 'they showed me'; the content clause introduced by 'che' contains 'nostra giustizia effetto sia del ciel' — 'nostra giustizia' is the topic ('our justice'), 'effetto' is predicate ('an effect/result'), and 'sia' is present subjunctive of essere used in indirect/reported content (a stylistic/optative or dependent-subjunctive choice in Dante). 'del ciel' = 'of the heaven'; 'che tu ingemme' is a relative clause modifying 'ciel' ('which you set with gems' / 'which you adorn with gems'), where 'tu' refers back to the addressed 'stella' and 'ingemme' = present of ingemma 'to set with gems, adorn.'
segments.paradiso-18-g118: Per ch’io prego = a fused introductory clause: Per (here 'for/therefore') + ch’ ('that') introduce the speaker's request. io prego is first-person present ('I entreat/ask'); prego carries the sense of entreaty rather than liturgical prayer. la mente is the direct object ('the mind' or intellectual faculty), and in che s’inizia is a relative clause: in che = 'in which' and s’inizia is a reflexive present form (si + inizia) meaning 'is begun / begins itself' — hence 'the mind in which your motion and your virtue begin.'
segments.paradiso-18-g119: che rimiri begins a subordinate relative/clause of purpose introduced by che ('that'); rimiri here is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'to contemplate/see' and is used with a subjunctive or desiderative force ('may behold'/'contemplate'). ond’ esce = ond' (whence/from which) + esce (present 'issues/comes out') and il fummo is the subject 'the smoke' (note: Dante uses fummo = 'fumo' smoke, an archaic spelling). the following che introduces a further relative clause modifying fummo: che ’l tuo raggio vizia = 'which corrupts/taints your ray' (vizia = present 'spoils/corrupts').
segments.paradiso-18-g120: sì ch’ = 'so that/with the result that' (introducing a result/consequence). un’altra fïata = 'another time/once again' (fïata = occasion); omai is an adverb 'now/by now', and s’adiri is si + adiri (reflexive) in a present form expressing result/potential ('may become angry/is moved to anger'). del comperare e vender treats the infinitives as nouns ('of the buying and selling'); dentro al templo = 'inside the temple' (dentro + al). che si murò uses che as a relative pronoun and si murò is a reflexive/passive past (murò) meaning 'was walled up/closed in', followed by di segni e di martìri 'with signs and martyrs' (i.e. adorned/filled with images and martyrs).
segments.paradiso-18-g121: A vocative address: 'O milizia del ciel' = 'O host (or militia) of heaven'. 'cu’' is an archaic relative/pronoun (cui/cu') meaning 'whom' or 'which' and introduces the relative clause; 'io' is the emphatic subject pronoun, and 'contemplo' is present indicative 'I contemplate' (subject follows the relative pronoun for emphasis). The whole segment names the addressee and immediately qualifies it by the speaker's act of contemplating it.
segments.paradiso-18-g122: 'adora' is an imperative (addressing the heavenly host) or present indicative used as exhortation — here a command: 'adore/worship'. 'per' introduces the beneficiary or purpose ('for/on behalf of'), 'color che sono in terra' is a relative clause 'those who are on earth', and 'tutti svïati dietro al malo essemplo' is a participial description: 'all led astray (svïati, a past participle used adjectivally) following (dietro) the bad example (essemplo, archaic spelling).' Word order places the command first, then the reason/purpose and the people affected.
segments.paradiso-18-g123: 'Già' here means 'formerly' or 'once'; 'si solea' is an impersonal reflexive construction with the imperfect 'solea' (used to be accustomed), literally 'one used to'; 'con le spade' is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'with the swords', and 'far guerra' (infinitive 'fare') means 'to make/wage war'. The impersonal 'si' makes the statement general rather than personal.
segments.paradiso-18-g124: 'ma or si fa' = 'but now it is done' (impersonal 'si' + present 'fa'). 'togliendo' is a gerund ('by taking away') that expresses the manner. The repeated 'or qui or quivi' is adverbial, 'now here now there' (poetic variability). 'lo pan' = 'the bread'; the relative clause 'che 'l pïo Padre a nessun serra' means literally 'which the pious Father closes to no one' — i.e. the Father refuses bread to no one. 'serra' is present tense 'closes' (used in a negative sense: denies).
segments.paradiso-18-g125: 'Ma tu' is a direct address ('But you'); 'che' introduces a relative clause 'who'; 'sol' here is archaic/poetic for 'solo/only' (meaning 'only' or 'merely') and modifies the purpose phrase; 'per cancellare' = 'in order to erase' (infinitive of purpose), and 'scrivi' is 2nd-person present 'you write' — the line criticises someone who writes merely to erase, i.e. to censor. Note: 'sol' in the supplied sense list does not include 'only', so that sense is irregular/archaic.
segments.paradiso-18-g126: 'pensa' is an imperative or exhortation meaning 'consider/think'; it takes a that-clause introduced by 'che' ('that Peter and Paul...'), and the two proper names are simply coordinated by 'e'. The construction asks the addressee to keep in mind those saints.
segments.paradiso-18-g127: The relative clause 'che moriro per la vigna che guasti' modifies 'Pietro e Paulo'. 'moriro' here is a poetic/historic form meaning 'died' (past idea, though listed generically as 'die'). 'per la vigna' gives the cause/reason 'for the vineyard', and the embedded relative 'che guasti' addresses the addressee: 'that you ruin' (second-person present). 'guasti' is a verb meaning 'you damage/ruin'.
segments.paradiso-18-g128: 'ancor' (archaic form of 'ancora') = 'still' or 'yet'; 'son' is the contracted form of 'sono' ('are'); 'vivi' is an adjective 'alive'. The short sentence affirms that, despite appearances, they remain alive (spiritually or in legacy).
segments.paradiso-18-g129: 'Ben puoi tu dire:' is an idiomatic opening meaning 'You can well/indeed say:' — 'Ben' (well/indeed) intensifies 'puoi' (you can), 'tu' is emphatic, and 'dire' is the infinitive 'to say'. This introduces a quoted hypothetical reply.
segments.paradiso-18-g130: Quoted first-person statement. 'I’ ho' = 'Io ho' (I have), with 'fermo' functioning as an adjective/past participle 'made firm' modifying '’l disiro' (the desire). 'sì a colui che volle viver solo' = 'fixed on him who willed to live alone' — 'volle' is a past-tense (historic/past) form of 'volere'. 'e che per salti fu tratto al martiro' = 'and who, by leaps/gaps, was drawn to martyrdom' — 'per salti' is an adverbial phrase ('by jumps/leaps'), 'fu tratto' is passive past ('was drawn'), and 'al martiro' 'to/into martyrdom.'
segments.paradiso-18-g131: The close of the quoted line: 'ch’io non conosco' = 'that I do not know' ('ch’' = che). 'il pescator' is 'the fisherman' (a common epithet for Saint Peter), 'né Polo' = 'nor Paul' (Polo = Paolo in medieval spelling). The speaker denies personal acquaintance with these figures. Note: some supplied sense lists use unusual lemmas for proper names; here 'Polo' refers to the person Paul rather than the literal pole.

sentences.paradiso-18-s9: And at the name of the high Maccabee I saw another move, spinning; and joy was Ferza of the paleo.
sentences.paradiso-18-s10: Thus, for Charlemagne and for Roland two of them my attentive gaze followed, as an eye follows its falcon flying.
sentences.paradiso-18-s11: Then William and Rinoard and the Duke Gottifredi drew my sight toward that cross, and Robert Guiscard.
sentences.paradiso-18-s12: Then, among the other lights, moved and mingled, the soul that had spoken to me showed itself—what it was among the singers of heaven, a master.
sentences.paradiso-18-s1: That blessed mirror was already enjoying only His Word, and I was savoring my own, tempering the bitter with the sweet; and that lady who was leading me to God said: “Change your thought; think that I am near to him who made light of every wrong.”
sentences.paradiso-18-s2: I turned myself to the loving sound of my encouragement; and just as then I saw Love in her holy eyes, so here is the abandonment (of self); not because I distrust my own words, but because the mind cannot so return upon itself, if another do not guide it.
sentences.paradiso-18-s3: I can recount so much from that moment that, contemplating her, my affection was free from every other desire, until the eternal pleasure, which directly shone in Beatrice, satisfied me from her fair face with a second look.
sentences.paradiso-18-s4: Overcoming me with the light of a smile, she said to me: “Turn and listen; for paradise is not only in my eyes.”
sentences.paradiso-18-s17: And as birds that have risen from the riverbank, almost as if rejoicing in their pasture, form themselves now a round, now another array, so within the lights holy creatures, flitting about, sang, and fashioned themselves now a D, now an I, now an L in their shapes.
sentences.paradiso-18-s18: At first, singing, they moved to their note; then, one of these signs becoming, they paused for a little and fell silent.
sentences.paradiso-18-s19: O Pegasean Muse, who makest the minds glorious and renderest them long‑lived, and who with thee (accompany) the cities and the realms, enlighten me about thee, so that I may bring out their forms as I conceived them: may thy power appear in these brief verses!
sentences.paradiso-18-s20: They therefore appear in five times seven vowels and consonants; and I noted the parts thus, as they seemed to me to have been said.
sentences.paradiso-18-s21: 'DILIGITE IUSTITIAM,' at first were the word and the name of the whole painting; 'QUI IUDICATIS TERRAM' were placed second.
sentences.paradiso-18-s22: Afterward the M's in the fifth word remained arranged; so that there Jupiter seemed silver, distinguished from gold.
sentences.paradiso-18-s23: And I saw other lights descend where the summit of the M was, and there to quiet themselves, singing — I believe — the good that moves them to itself.
sentences.paradiso-18-s24: Then, as when, in striking burning blocks, countless sparks rise up (from which fools are wont to draw omens), there seemed to spring up from there more than a thousand lights and to ascend, some greatly and some little, just as the sun that kindles them makes them come forth; and, each calmed in its own place, I saw the head and neck of an eagle represented in that distinct flame.
sentences.paradiso-18-s5: As one sometimes sees here a feeling in the eyes — if it is so strong that it takes the whole soul away from the person — so, in the flashing of the holy lightning at which I turned, I perceived in it the desire to speak with me a little more.
sentences.paradiso-18-s6: He began: "On this fifth threshold of the tree that lives at its summit and ever bears fruit and never loses a leaf, there are blessed spirits, who, when they were below before they came to heaven, were of great voice, so that every muse would be enriched by it."
sentences.paradiso-18-s7: Therefore look at the arms (horns) of the cross: that which I will name there will perform the act that his swift fire does in a cloud.
sentences.paradiso-18-s8: I saw, along the cross, a light drawn from the naming 'Joshua', as he himself made it; nor was the saying known to me before the deed.
sentences.paradiso-18-s13: I turned to my right side to look at Beatrice and see my duty toward her — whether to speak or to make a sign — and I saw her lights so pure, so joyful, that her appearance outshone the others and even the farthest sun.
sentences.paradiso-18-s14: And just as a man, to feel more delight in doing good, notices from day to day that his virtue advances, so I perceived that my turning about together with the sky had enlarged the arc, beholding that more splendid miracle.
sentences.paradiso-18-s15: And just as changing in a brief interval of time into a fair lady, when her face is relieved of the burden of shame, so it was in my eyes when I was turned, through the whiteness of the tempered sixth star that had gathered me within itself.
sentences.paradiso-18-s16: I saw in that jovial little torch the sparkling of the love that was there, marking to my eyes our speech.
sentences.paradiso-18-s25: He who paints there has no one to guide him; but he himself guides, and from him that virtue is remembered which is the form for the nests.
sentences.paradiso-18-s26: The other beatitude, which at first seemed content to entangle itself in the letter M, followed the imprint with little motion.
sentences.paradiso-18-s27: O sweet star, what kinds and how many gems showed me that our justice is an effect of the heaven which you adorn with gems!
sentences.paradiso-18-s28: Therefore I entreat the mind in which your motion and your virtue begin, that it may behold whence comes the smoke that corrupts your ray; so that once again now it may be angered at the buying and selling inside the temple that was walled up with signs and martyrs.
sentences.paradiso-18-s29: O host of heaven whom I contemplate, adore on behalf of those who are on earth, all led astray following the bad example!
sentences.paradiso-18-s30: Once it was customary to make war with swords; but now it is done by taking away, now here, now there, the bread which the pious Father denies to no one.
sentences.paradiso-18-s31: But you, who write only to erase, think that Peter and Paul who died for the vineyard that you ruin are still alive.
sentences.paradiso-18-s32: You can well say: "I have set my desire firmly on him who chose to live alone and who, by leaps, was drawn to martyrdom, that I do not know the fisherman nor Paul."
